keyMyths.title: Janus and Camese
keyMyths.description: Janus, while ruling Latium, welcomed the nymph Camese, who was fleeing from Picus. He hid her from the king and she later bore him children.

keyMyths.title: Janus Opens the Gates of War
keyMyths.description: During times of war, the doors of Janus' temple were opened to signify that Roman legions were marching out to battle; in times of peace, they were closed to signify peace throughout the Roman empire.

keyMyths.title: Janus and the Sabines
keyMyths.description: When the Sabines attacked Rome, Janus caused a hot spring to erupt, scalding the invaders and driving them back, thus saving the city from capture.

keyMyths.title: Janus' Hospitality
keyMyths.description: Saturn, fleeing from Jupiter, was welcomed by Janus in Italy. Janus shared his kingdom with Saturn and taught the people agriculture and civilization.

keyMyths.title: The Temple of Janus
keyMyths.description: The Temple of Janus was not a true temple, but a bronze enclosure with doors on each end, representing the god's ability to look both forward and backward. Its opening and closing dictated the state of war or peace in Rome.

sources.author: Ovid
